skill.name: android-native-dev
skill.description: Guides Android native app development with Kotlin, Jetpack Compose, Material Design 3, accessibility, testing, performance, and build troubleshooting practices.
skill.summary: Build and review Android native apps using Kotlin, Compose, Material Design 3, and Android quality guidance.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, storage, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows they are primarily Markdown guidance, Kotlin examples, Material Design terminology, and security recommendations. No malicious intent, credential exfiltration, prompt injection, or executable helper script was found. The remaining risk is operational: the skill instructs agents to run Gradle, ADB, and test commands, so users should review commands before execution.

medium_findings.title: Agent May Run Build and Device Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill provides Gradle and ADB command examples for building, installing, launching, and testing Android apps. These are legitimate Android development workflows, but they can modify the workspace, download dependencies, or interact with a connected device when executed by an agent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and actionable, but they are standard Android development commands in Markdown documentation. I found no evidence that the skill executes them automatically.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Alerts Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby or shell backtick execution alerts come from Markdown inline code, Kotlin test names, Gradle examples, and documentation tables. They do not indicate executable Ruby code or command injection inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are prose and fenced Markdown examples, not runnable skill scripts. The repository contains Markdown files and no executable helper files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Mostly Terminology or Avoidance Guidance
low_findings.description: Weak-crypto detections are triggered by terms such as Material Design 3, MD-style references, numeric design ratios, and security guidance that explicitly warns users to avoid MD5, SHA1, hardcoded keys, and custom cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is defensive guidance or design terminology. I found no code that implements weak cryptography or recommends weak algorithms for security use.

low_findings.title: Network and Storage Alerts Are Documentation Context
low_findings.description: Network and storage detections appear in Android architecture, testing, and privacy guidance. The examples discuss API calls, HTTPS requirements, internal storage, encrypted preferences, and test reports rather than exfiltration or hidden file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines describe standard Android app concerns and safer handling practices. I found no external endpoint used by the skill to transmit user data.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: A targeted search for instruction override phrases found no evidence of prompt injection attempts. Matches for override are ordinary Kotlin method declarations in examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious phrase search returned only normal Kotlin override methods. No text claims special authority, asks the evaluator to skip analysis, or attempts to override system instructions.

content.value_statement: Android projects often fail because setup, UI quality, accessibility, and testing are handled late. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured Android native development checklist.
actual_capabilities: Guides project setup for Gradle, AndroidX, Kotlin, Compose, and build variants.
Provides Material Design 3 rules for color, typography, spacing, touch targets, and app style.
Explains accessibility requirements for labels, focus order, screen readers, and contrast testing.
Covers performance and stability checks for startup, ANR prevention, memory, and Android Vitals.
Documents testing patterns for unit tests, Robolectric, Espresso, Compose UI tests, and managed devices.
Includes privacy and security guidance for permissions, storage, network security, WebView, and cryptography.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not provide executable Android project templates.
Some dependency versions are examples and may need updates for a specific project.
It cannot verify a real device, emulator, or Play Console status without tool access.
It does not replace official Android documentation for policy or platform changes.

use_cases.title: Start a New Android App
use_cases.description: Use the setup checklist to create a working Gradle and Compose foundation before adding product logic.

use_cases.title: Review UI Quality Before Release
use_cases.description: Check screens against Material Design 3, accessibility, adaptive layout, and performance requirements.

use_cases.title: Fix Build and Test Failures
use_cases.description: Use the troubleshooting sections to diagnose Gradle errors, dependency conflicts, and Android test setup gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Create Project Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new Android app
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the android-native-dev skill to list the files, Gradle settings, and first build checks needed for a new Kotlin Compose Android app.

prompt_templates.title: Review Compose Screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the android-native-dev skill to review this Compose screen for Material Design 3, accessibility, spacing, and state handling issues.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Build Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the android-native-dev skill to analyze this Gradle error and propose the smallest safe fix. Explain what command should verify the fix.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Release Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the android-native-dev skill to create a release readiness review covering tests, Android Vitals, privacy, permissions, adaptive screens, and accessibility.

output_examples.input: Review a Compose login screen for release quality.
output: Flags missing content descriptions and weak focus order.
Checks button height, spacing, contrast, and typography against Material Design 3 guidance.
Suggests a concise verification pass using Compose UI tests and accessibility checks.

output_examples.input: Help fix an Android build with duplicate classes.
output: Identifies dependency conflict as the likely cause.
Recommends inspecting the dependency tree before changing versions.
Defines a verification step using a clean debug build.

output_examples.input: Prepare a testing plan for an existing app.
output: Separates local unit tests, Robolectric tests, Espresso tests, Compose UI tests, and managed device tests.
Maps each layer to the app behavior it should cover.
Notes that test dependencies should match the project version catalog.

best_practices: Run a successful debug build before adding complex application logic.
Review UI changes against accessibility, adaptive layout, and performance requirements together.
Inspect existing Gradle versions before adding dependencies or test libraries.
anti_patterns: Do not add test dependencies or plugin versions without checking the project version catalog.
Do not ignore accessibility labels, touch targets, contrast, or screen reader behavior until release.
Do not execute Gradle, ADB, or device commands without confirming the target project and device context.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build and review native Android apps using Kotlin, Jetpack Compose, Gradle, and Material Design 3 guidance.

faq.question: Does it create a complete app automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides development guidance, checklists, examples, and troubleshooting patterns for an agent to apply in a project.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Does it cover accessibility?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers content descriptions, focus order, touch targets, contrast, screen readers, keyboard navigation, and accessibility testing.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers permissions, encrypted storage, HTTPS, WebView safety, cryptography, component exports, and Google Play data policy.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation, but it includes Gradle and ADB commands that can modify projects or connected devices when executed.
